prompt:
I just spent a session rewriting the ETRO essay content. The
`src/app/(frontend)/api/update-etro/route.ts` file has the new blurb,
scope statement, and section bodies I wanted. But the live site still
shows the old content. The file was saved successfully, no errors. What's
going on, and what should the workflow have been so I don't run into
this every time we do content work?
response:
I now have a complete picture of the problem, the catalogued anti-pattern, and the guardrail. Here is the full diagnosis.

---

## Diagnosis

**The content you wrote never reached the database.** The live site is not wrong — it is faithfully displaying what Payload CMS has stored. Your edits went into the wrong place in the content pipeline and were never actually applied.

---

## Root Cause — **EAP-087: Modifying a Content Seeding Route Without Calling the Endpoint**

This is a catalogued, recurring anti-pattern with its own hard guardrail (Engineering Guardrail #25). Here is exactly what happened and why it's non-obvious:

### The architecture of content on this site

```
route.ts file (source of intent)
        ↓  [HTTP call required]
Payload CMS database (source of truth)
        ↓  [fetched at render time]
Live website (what users see)
```

`src/app/(frontend)/api/update-etro/route.ts` is **not a configuration file, a template, or a static data store.** It is a one-shot imperative script — a seeding route — that contains TypeScript constants describing desired CMS state. When the route receives an HTTP `POST`, it calls `getPayload()`, constructs a Lexical document from your markdown, and calls `payload.update()` to write that data into the database. The constants in the file are just function arguments. **They do nothing by themselves.**

When you edited `BLURB_BODY` and `CONTENT_BLOCKS` and saved the file, you updated the arguments to a function. You never called the function. The database was never touched. The site continued serving the old CMS data because that data was never overwritten.

This is exactly ENG-149 in the feedback log — *"ETRO essay content rewrite not visible on website - seeding endpoint never called"* — the same project, the same file, the same failure mode.

---

## The Fix — What to Do Right Now

You have two options depending on your current environment:

### Option A — Call the endpoint directly (dev server running)

```bash
# Verify your dev server is up first
curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" http://localhost:4000

# Then POST to the seeding route
curl -X POST http://localhost:4000/api/update-etro
```

Watch for a `200` response with a success payload. If you get anything else, check the dev server terminal for Payload errors (schema mismatches, Lexical serialization issues, etc.).

### Option B — Via the Next.js route handler (browser)

Navigate to `http://localhost:4000/api/update-etro` and trigger a POST. If the route is GET-accessible for convenience, a browser visit may suffice — but check the route handler definition:

```typescript
// src/app/(frontend)/api/update-etro/route.ts
export async function POST(...)   // requires curl -X POST
// vs.
export async function GET(...)    // browser-navigable
```

Adapt accordingly. After the call succeeds, hard-refresh the live ETRO page and confirm your new blurb, scope statement, and section bodies are visible.

---

## The Correct Workflow for Every Content Session

This is the mandatory 3-step pattern enforced by **Engineering Guardrail #25**:

```
Step 1 — EDIT the route file
         Write your new content into BLURB_BODY, CONTENT_BLOCKS, etc.
         (What you did ✓)

Step 2 — CALL the endpoint
         curl -X POST http://localhost:<port>/api/update-<slug>
         Verify HTTP 200 + success body
         (What was skipped ✗)

Step 3 — VERIFY on the live page
         Hard-refresh the page in a non-Safari browser
         Confirm the new text is present in the rendered HTML
         (Blocked by Step 2 being skipped)
```

The guardrail exists precisely because Step 2 is invisible — the file saves cleanly, no errors are thrown, nothing in the editor signals that anything is wrong. The only feedback is that the website doesn't change.

### Why this keeps happening

The mental model that causes the mistake is: *"I edited the content file, so the content changed."* That model is correct for static sites, JSON data files, and MDX — but it is wrong here. This site's content is stored in a Postgres database managed by Payload. The route file is the **write script**, not the **data store**. Editing the script without running it is equivalent to editing a SQL migration file without running `db:migrate`.

A useful heuristic: **if a file lives under `src/app/(frontend)/api/update-*/`, it requires an HTTP call after every edit.** The file name contains the word `update` for this reason.

---

## Principle

> **The seeding route is a write script, not a data store. Editing it changes the intent; calling it changes the database. Only the database change is visible on the site.** Always close the loop: edit → call → verify. (EAP-087 / Engineering Guardrail #25)
